findings.summary: Stage list is copy-pasted across execute_full_pipeline, execute_core_pipeline, and _resume_pipeline rather than composed from a shared definition
evidence: Lines 146-170 (execute_full_pipeline) and 183-204 (execute_core_pipeline) both construct stages lists with identical entries for Clean, Environment Setup, Infrastructure Tests, Project Tests, Analysis, PDF Rendering, Output Validation, and Copy Outputs
_resume_pipeline (lines 340-359) re-builds yet a third variant of the same stage list inline, adding conditional LLM stages
Any addition or rename of a stage requires touching all three sites
findings.suggestion: Extract a single _build_stage_list(include_llm: bool, skip_clean: bool) method that returns the canonical stage list. All three entry points call it, and only the flag arguments vary.

findings.summary: Exit-code-2 (graceful skip) is handled inside _resume_pipeline but not inside _execute_pipeline, splitting the same concern across two call sites
evidence: _resume_pipeline lines 416-424 explicitly checks exit_code == 2 and rebuilds a new PipelineStageResult with success=True
_execute_pipeline (lines 206-243) has no such handling; callers of execute_full_pipeline/execute_core_pipeline do not get the same skip promotion

_run_llm_review/_run_llm_translations call _run_script with allow_skip_code=True, which internally returns True for code 2; but _execute_stage captures only the bool, so exit_code is always stored as 0 or 1 (line 288-293) — the PipelineStageResult.exit_code therefore never equals 2 in the non-resume path, making the resume-path check dead code
findings.suggestion: Centralise skip-code promotion inside _execute_stage: accept an allow_skip_code parameter (passed through from stage spec tuples) and promote there. Remove the redundant post-hoc fixup in _resume_pipeline.

findings.summary: PipelineExecutor manually manages root-logger file handlers (add, scan, remove, re-add after clean), which should be an infrastructure logging concern, not a pipeline orchestration concern
evidence: Lines 88-133: _setup_log_file_handler iterates root_logger.handlers, searches by baseFilename, closes and removes old handlers, then appends a new one
Lines 470-486: _run_clean_outputs calls _setup_log_file_handler a second time mid-pipeline to re-attach after the clean stage deletes the directory
Lines 562-598: _flush_log_handlers iterates root_logger.handlers and every named logger's handlers to flush before copy — this is global logger state manipulation inside a pipeline class
findings.suggestion: Move the log-file lifecycle into a LogFileManager or extend setup_logger in logging_utils.py to support rotate-on-recreate. PipelineExecutor calls a single LogFileManager.attach(path) / LogFileManager.reattach() without touching the root logger directly.

findings.summary: performance.py and performance_monitor.py both export a symbol named monitor_performance with incompatible signatures, creating a silent naming collision at the module boundary
evidence: performance.py line 422: monitor_performance = performance_context (a context manager alias)
performance_monitor.py lines 267-289: monitor_performance is a decorator factory taking (operation_name, track_memory)

An importer doing 'from infrastructure.core.performance import monitor_performance' gets the context manager; from performance_monitor gets the decorator — the same exported name means different things at each boundary
findings.suggestion: Rename the alias in performance.py to performance_context only (remove the monitor_performance alias), leaving monitor_performance exclusively as the decorator in performance_monitor.py. Update callers.

findings.summary: render_all in RenderManager appends to outputs only on success but catches sub-renderer exceptions and continues, so callers receive a list that may be shorter than expected with no indication of which renderers failed
evidence: Lines 77-107: Beamer slide failure is caught, logged as warning, and execution continues — nothing is appended to outputs
Lines 110-116: HTML failure is similarly caught and swallowed
Line 118: 'if not outputs: raise TemplateError' only triggers when ALL renderers fail; partial failure is silently returned as a shorter list
Callers have no way to distinguish '1 format succeeded' from '2 formats succeeded' without inspecting file names
findings.suggestion: Return a RenderResult dataclass containing both succeeded paths and per-renderer failure reasons, or at minimum raise TemplateError when any configured renderer fails unless explicitly marked optional.

findings.summary: StagePerformanceTracker stores start_memory and start_io as instance attributes set inside start_stage rather than as method-local or per-stage dataclass, making the object stateful in a fragile way
evidence: Lines 267-281: start_stage sets self.start_memory and self.start_io (never declared in __init__)
end_stage (line 284) accesses self.start_io without guarding for the case where start_stage was never called beyond 'if self.start_time is None: return {}'
If start_stage raises after self.start_time is set but before self.start_io is set, end_stage would raise AttributeError on self.start_io
__init__ (lines 262-265) initialises only self.stages and self.start_time; start_memory and start_io are absent
findings.suggestion: Declare self.start_memory: float = 0.0 and self.start_io: Optional[Any] = None in __init__, or bundle per-stage start data into a local _StageSnapshot dataclass stored as one attribute.

findings.summary: ProfilingMetrics.__post_init__ mutates Optional[int] fields in-place using integer division, silently discarding sub-MB precision and relying on the caller to not re-read the raw byte values
evidence: Lines 37-44: if self.memory_peak: self.memory_peak = self.memory_peak // (1024*1024) converts bytes to MB destructively
The field is typed Optional[int] (bytes semantics before __post_init__, MB semantics after) — the same field name carries two different units depending on lifecycle phase
to_dict() at line 53 labels the key 'memory_peak_mb' suggesting MB, but downstream code reading .memory_peak directly sees MB not bytes — unit ambiguity embedded in the type
findings.suggestion: Store raw bytes in a private _memory_peak_bytes field and expose a @property memory_peak_mb -> Optional[float] that converts on access. The dataclass fields should have stable, single-unit semantics.

findings.summary: verify_cross_references sets all five integrity keys to False when any missing label is found, making the per-type breakdown it returns meaningless
evidence: Lines 96-102: integrity dict is initialised with per-type keys: equations, figures, tables, sections, citations
Lines 129-133: missing_labels check does 'integrity = {k: False for k in integrity}' — all keys go False regardless of which reference type failed
The fine-grained dict structure implies the caller can determine which reference type is broken, but the implementation collapses everything to a uniform False
findings.suggestion: Map reference label prefixes (e.g., 'fig:', 'eq:', 'tab:') to their integrity keys and set only the affected keys to False, or simplify the return to a single bool and remove the misleading per-type dict.

findings.summary: The fallback model loop in LLMClient.query references start_time which is not defined in that scope
evidence: Line 257: generation_time = time_module.time() - start_time inside the except LLMConnectionError fallback loop

start_time is never assigned in the query() method; the only timing variable is response_text, generation_time from _time_call() at line 226-228, which is inside the try block and would not be in scope if the exception was raised before that assignment
This means the fallback path will raise NameError at line 257 when it succeeds on a fallback model
findings.suggestion: Capture start_time = time_module.time() at the top of query() before the try block, consistent with how it is used in query_raw() and other methods.

findings.summary: performance.py and performance_monitor.py serve the same domain (timing and memory measurement) with overlapping classes and no clear ownership boundary
evidence: performance.py exports PerformanceMonitor (resource tracker), PerformanceMetrics, ResourceUsage, StagePerformanceTracker, performance_context
performance_monitor.py exports CodeProfiler, ProfilingMetrics, monitor_performance decorator, benchmark_function, profile_memory_usage
Both modules use psutil for process memory; both wrap tracemalloc; both expose context managers for timing

performance.py module docstring (line 4) explicitly says 'For function-level profiling and timing decorators, see performance_monitor.py' — the boundary is acknowledged but the implementations overlap rather than compose
findings.suggestion: Consolidate into one module or make the split strict: performance.py owns pipeline-stage granularity (StagePerformanceTracker only) and performance_monitor.py owns function/code granularity (CodeProfiler only), with no shared psutil wrappers.

findings.summary: generate_pipeline_report accepts 9 parameters, 6 of which are Optional, making call sites verbose and the function boundary diffuse
evidence: Lines 277-287: signature is (stage_results, total_duration, repo_root, test_results=None, validation_results=None, performance_metrics=None, error_summary=None, output_statistics=None, project_name=None)
The function body (lines 303-338) handles each optional separately with ad-hoc mutation (lines 318-325 mutate output_statistics in-place to add log_file info)
Callers must pass positional + keyword soup; the large optional set is a signal these fields belong in a config/input dataclass
findings.suggestion: Introduce a ReportInput dataclass (or extend PipelineReport as the input type) so callers construct it with named fields and pass one object. The function signature becomes (input: ReportInput, repo_root: Path) -> PipelineReport.

findings.summary: CheckpointManager resolves the default checkpoint directory by walking up from __file__, coupling the module's filesystem location to runtime path resolution
evidence: Lines 77-79: repo_root = Path(__file__).parent.parent.parent followed by checkpoint_dir = repo_root / 'projects' / project_name / ...
This assumes checkpoint.py is always exactly 3 directory levels below the repo root; moving the file or running from an installed package breaks the path silently
PipelineExecutor already holds config.repo_root (pipeline.py line 76); it passes only project_name to CheckpointManager, forcing CheckpointManager to re-derive repo_root itself
findings.suggestion: Add an explicit repo_root: Optional[Path] = None parameter to CheckpointManager.__init__ and pass config.repo_root from PipelineExecutor. Remove the __file__-relative derivation.

findings.summary: performance_context yields the monitor to the caller but also calls monitor.stop() in the finally block, so any caller that calls monitor.stop() explicitly (as shown in the docstring example) will get a RuntimeError on the second call
evidence: Lines 210-221: performance_context yields monitor; finally block calls monitor.stop() unconditionally

Docstring example at lines 203-208 shows '... metrics = monitor.stop()' inside the with block — this is the documented usage, but it will cause stop() to be called twice: once by the caller, once by finally

PerformanceMonitor.stop() at line 108 raises RuntimeError('Monitor not started') if start_time is None; after first stop(), start_time is not reset so the second stop() would also fail with the same error
findings.suggestion: Either yield the monitor without calling stop() in finally (let the caller decide), or do not yield the monitor at all and capture metrics internally. The current API promises both managed lifetime and caller control, which conflict.
